1: wayyēleḵ rĕḥaḇʿām šĕḵemāh — Rehoboam went to Shechem for the coronation assembly. Shechem (šĕḵem, H7927) was not his capital but the covenant assembly site — the place where Joshua renewed the covenant (Josh 24) and where Abimelech made his bid for kingship (Judg 9). The choice of Shechem signals that Rehoboam knew he needed northern ratification, not merely Judahite succession. The assembly (qāhal, H6951) functions as the covenant community gathered to confirm the king; Israelite kingship required the people’s consent expressed through the tribal representatives. The Deuteronomic law of the king (Deut 17:14-20) presupposes this dynamic: the king is set up by the community, not only by dynastic succession.
2: Jeroboam son of Nebat was in Egypt, where he had fled from Solomon (cf. 1 Kgs 11:40). The note that Jeroboam was still in Egypt when news arrived is the narrative preparation for the contrast between the two responses to Rehoboam’s question: the elders who served Solomon (vv. 6-7) and the young men who grew up with Rehoboam (vv. 8-11). Jeroboam’s Egyptian exile is the political background — he had already received the prophetic word from Ahijah (11:29-39) about the ten tribes; his return is the fulfillment in process.
3: The assembly summoned Jeroboam and brought him back; he came before Rehoboam with the whole assembly of Israel. The qāhal kol-yiśrāʾēl (whole assembly of Israel) is the formal covenant body — not a popular crowd but the representative covenant gathering that has legal authority to make covenantal demands of the incoming king. Their request in v.4 is a formal petition from the covenant assembly, not a complaint from disgruntled workers. The presence of Jeroboam alongside the assembly signals that he is their representative voice and potential alternative.
4: ʾāḇîḵā hiqqšāh ʾeṯ-ʿullēnû wĕʿaṯṯāh hāqēl mēʿăḇōḏaṯ ʾāḇîḵā — Your father made our yoke hard; lighten the servitude of your father. The ʿōl (H5923, yoke) and ʿăḇōḏāh (H5656, servitude/labor) are the vocabulary of the Egyptian bondage (cf. Exod 1:14; 6:6-9 where both terms describe Pharaoh’s oppression). The assembly is deliberately invoking the exodus: Solomon’s corvée state had replicated the conditions Israel was delivered from. The request is for covenant relief — a return to the servant-king model where the king serves the people (v.7) rather than enslaving them. The adjective qāšāh (H7185, hard/harsh) in hiqqšāh ʾeṯ-ʿullēnû is the same word used for Pharaoh’s hardening (Exod 13:15).
5: Rehoboam asks for three days: lĕḵû ʿôḏ šĕlōšāh yāmîm. The three-day deliberation period is a standard ancient Near Eastern consultation interval — not a legal term but a recognized period for taking counsel. Rehoboam’s delay is prudent protocol, but it will be poorly used. The parallel with 1 Kgs 12:12 (the people returned on the third day as the king had told them) creates the structure of a formal hearing with deliberation followed by verdict. The interval is a gift he squanders by consulting the wrong advisors.
6: Rehoboam consults the zĕqēnîm (H2205, elders) who had stood before (ʿāmĕdû lipnê) Solomon during his lifetime. ʿāmad lipnê (to stand before) is the technical term for royal service (cf. 1 Kgs 1:2; 10:8; 12:8) — these are experienced court officials who had observed Solomon’s administration firsthand. The elders’ advice in v.7 articulates the servant-king ideal of Deuteronomy 17: the king who is not lifted up above his brothers (Deut 17:20), who reads YHWH’s law daily (17:19) and learns the fear of YHWH. They are recommending the covenant constitution of Israelite kingship.
7: ʾim-hayyôm tihyeh-ʿeḇeḏ lāʿam hazzeh ûḵĕḇaltô waʿănîtām wĕdibbartā ʾălêhem dĕḇārîm ṭôḇîm — If today you will be a servant to this people and serve them and answer them with good words, they will be your servants forever. The elders’ advice centers on the ʿeḇeḏ (H5650, servant) language — the king who serves his people is the biblical ideal. dĕḇārîm ṭôḇîm (good words) stands in contrast to the harsh word Rehoboam will actually give (v.13: qāšāh). The conditional promise — wĕhāyû lĕḵā ʿăḇādîm kol-hayyāmîm (they will be your servants forever) — shows that genuine servant-leadership generates willing loyalty, not the forced service that breaks it.
8: Rehoboam rejected (wayyaʿăzōḇ, H5800, abandoned/forsook) the advice of the elders and consulted the young men (hayyelādîm, H3206, boys/youths) who had grown up with him. The verb ʿāzaḇ (to forsake/abandon) is used extensively in DtrH for abandoning YHWH (1 Kgs 11:33; 14:22; 18:18) — its use here for forsaking the elders’ counsel is theologically loaded. Rehoboam abandons wisdom as the unfaithful kings will abandon YHWH. The young men who “grew up with him” (gāḏĕlû ʾittô) are courtiers of his own peer-cohort, lacking the formation that comes from witnessing the consequences of policy over time.
9: Rehoboam’s question to the young men is identical to his question to the elders: “what do you advise that we answer this people?” The parallel question generates the contrasting answers that define the chapter’s theological claim. The fact that Rehoboam asks both groups the same question in the same words shows that his failure is not informational — he received the same inquiry both times — but dispositional: he was already inclined toward the answer he received from the young men.
10: qāṭān mimmennî yigbah mimmōṯnê ʾāḇî — My little finger is thicker than my father’s loins/waist. The gross anatomical boast of the young men is one of the most arrogant statements in the Hebrew Bible. qāṭān (H6996, small/little) in qāṭān mimmennî refers to the little finger — the least significant part of the king. The comparison of the king’s little finger to his father’s loins (mōṯnayim, H4975, the seat of strength) is a deliberate assertion of superiority through anatomical vulgarity. The young men are advising not merely firmness but contemptuous dominance.
11: wĕʿaṯṯāh ʾāḇî ʾibbas ʾĕṯḵem bašśôṭîm wăʾănî ʾăyasēr ʾĕṯḵem bāʿaqrabbîm — My father disciplined you with whips; I will discipline you with scorpions. The contrast between šôṭîm (H7752, whips) and ʿaqrabbîm (H6137, scorpions) escalates the image of punishment. ʿaqrabbîm may refer to a barbed or spiked whip, not actual scorpions (cf. the scorpion metaphor in Ezek 2:6 for the stubborn people). The rhetorical function is the claim to surpass Solomon’s harshness by an order of magnitude. This is the language of the despot, not the shepherd-king — precisely the failure of Israelite kingship that the Deuteronomic law warned against (Deut 17:20: the king must not think himself above his brothers).
12: Jeroboam and all the people returned on the third day, as the king had instructed. The formal structure of the hearing is preserved: the assembly had been sent away with a time limit, they return punctually, and await the royal verdict. The careful protocol of the assembly contrasts with the contempt about to be shown to it. The narrator’s point-by-point parallel (v.12 echoing v.5) structures the scene as a formal covenant proceeding with a disastrous verdict.
13: wayyaʿănēm hammelek qāšāh — the king answered the people harshly. qāšāh (H7185) is the harsh-hardness of Pharaoh’s oppression and the stubborn neck of the rebellious people. The narrator’s summary before the specific words — “rejecting the advice of the elders” — is a theological verdict. The zeqēnîm (elders) embody accumulated covenant wisdom; wayyaʿăzōḇ (he rejected/abandoned) anticipates the apostasy-language that will characterize the northern kingdom’s relationship with YHWH. The king who abandons wisdom for flattery will produce a kingdom that abandons YHWH for idols.
14: He spoke according to the advice of the young men: my father loaded your yoke heavy; I will add to it. The exact repetition of the young men’s words (vv.10-11) in the king’s formal answer is the narrator’s technique of showing Rehoboam’s complete capture by his peer-cohort. He has not weighed the advice and chosen; he has simply repeated what he was told. The king who cannot distinguish between advisors — who cannot evaluate counsel according to covenant wisdom — is the king the DtrH is showing to be unfit. The whip-and-scorpion language in a formal covenant proceeding is the declaration that this king does not understand the covenant relationship between king and people.
15: kî-hāyĕṯāh sĕḇîḇāh mēʿim YHWH lĕmaʿan hāqîm ʾeṯ-dĕḇārô — for this turn of events was from YHWH, to establish/fulfill his word. The narrator interrupts the political narrative with a theological explanation: the sĕḇîḇāh (H5438, turn of events/cause) is YHWH’s sovereign action. lĕmaʿan hāqîm (in order to establish/fulfill) marks divine purpose behind historical contingency. The word being established is Ahijah’s prophecy to Jeroboam (11:29-39). The hardening of Pharaoh’s heart to establish the Exodus is the theological precedent: YHWH works through human stubbornness to accomplish his decreed purpose. Rehoboam’s folly is real; it is also YHWH’s instrument.
16: mah-lānû ḥēleq bĕḏāwid wĕlōʾ-naḥălāh bĕḇen-yišāy lĕʾohāleḵā yiśrāʾēl — What share do we have in David? What inheritance in the son of Jesse? The war-cry of secession echoes Sheba son of Bichri’s cry in 2 Sam 20:1 — nearly word for word. ḥēleq (H2506, portion/share) and naḥălāh (H5159, inheritance/portion) are covenant-land terms: the ten tribes are declaring that they have no covenantal stake in the Davidic house. “To your tents, O Israel” is the ancient dispersal-formula after assembly (cf. 1 Kgs 8:66); here it signals permanent dissolution rather than mere dismissal.
17: Rehoboam still reigned over the Israelites who were living in the cities of Judah. The contracted scope of the Davidic kingship — now “the cities of Judah” rather than “all Israel and Judah” — is the immediate consequence of the folly. The note is transitional but theologically significant: the dynasty that was promised the eternal covenant (2 Sam 7) now governs a truncated territory. The covenant promise remains; its scope is provisionally narrowed by covenant faithlessness.
18: Rehoboam sent Adoram (ʾadōrām) — the official in charge of forced labor (hammās, H4522) — into the north; the people stoned him to death. The sending of the corvée-overseer rather than a diplomat or conciliator as the first post-declaration contact is the height of political obtuseness. mammās (forced levy/conscript labor) was the very grievance driving the secession; Rehoboam’s response is to send the officer who represents the most resented aspect of the policy. The people’s response — stoning (rāgam, H7275) — was the covenant penalty for crimes against the assembly. Rehoboam flees in his chariot.
19: wayyipšĕʿû yiśrāʾēl bĕḇêṯ dāwid ʿaḏ hayyôm hazzeh — Israel has been in rebellion against the house of David to this day. pāšaʿ (H6586, to rebel/transgress) is both political rebellion and covenant transgression — the same verb used for Israel’s rebellion against YHWH (Isa 1:2). The narrator’s “to this day” (ʿaḏ hayyôm hazzeh) connects the narrator’s own time to this founding moment of the division — written after the exile, looking back at the ongoing rupture. The division is not presented as merely political but as an act of covenant transgression that characterizes the northern kingdom throughout its existence.
20: The assembly of all Israel summoned Jeroboam and installed him as king over all Israel; none followed the house of David except the tribe of Judah alone. The wayyaqhîlû (assembled him, from qāhal) echoes the covenant assembly vocabulary; Jeroboam’s accession is formally parallel to Rehoboam’s — both receive assembly-ratification. The exception note — “only the tribe of Judah” — preserves the Davidic line with its promise but reduces its territorial scope drastically. The narrator’s framing “all Israel” vs. “only the tribe of Judah” is the DtrH’s account of the covenant people’s permanent division.
21: Rehoboam assembled 180,000 soldiers to fight against the house of Israel and restore the kingdom to Rehoboam. The number 180,000 (me’at ûšĕmōnîm ʾālep̄) — whether literal or rhetorical — represents the full military mobilization of Judah and Benjamin. The stated purpose is restoration of the united kingdom by force — a legitimate dynastic goal but one YHWH will explicitly prohibit through the prophetic word.
22: The word of God (dĕḇar hāʾĕlōhîm) came to Shemaiah the man of God (ʾîš hāʾĕlōhîm). The divine word through the prophetic figure interrupts the military mobilization — YHWH’s word is the sovereign check on royal action throughout Kings. ʾîš hāʾĕlōhîm (H376 + H430) is the title for the authoritative divine messenger (cf. Moses, Deut 33:1; Samuel, 1 Sam 9:6; Elijah and Elisha throughout). Shemaiah is otherwise unknown; the DtrH preserves the name to authenticate the oracle’s prophetic origin.
23: The oracle is directed at “Rehoboam son of Solomon, king of Judah, and all the house of Judah and Benjamin, and the rest of the people.” The specific address to all parties — not only Rehoboam but the whole mobilized force — is the covenantal scope of the prohibition. The divine word does not merely advise the king; it commands the entire assembly. The inclusiveness of the address shows that YHWH’s sovereign direction applies to the community as a whole, not only its leaders.
24: lōʾ-taʿălû wĕlōʾ-tiilāḥămû ʿim-ʾăḥêḵem bĕnê-yiśrāʾēl — Do not go up and do not fight against your brothers, the sons of Israel. The prohibition uses ʾăḥêḵem (your brothers) — the covenant kinship term. The division is YHWH’s work (kî mēʾittî hāyĕṯāh haddāḇār hazzeh, this thing is from me): YHWH prohibits the military reversal of what he himself ordained. The obedience of the people — they went home according to the divine word — is remarkable in a military mobilization context. The divine word spoken through the prophet has legal authority that overrides the royal military command.
25: Jeroboam built (wayyiḇen) Shechem in the hill country of Ephraim and settled there; he also built Penuel. The establishment of administrative cities — Shechem (central, near the covenant assembly site) and Penuel (Transjordanian, east of the Jordan) — is Jeroboam’s political consolidation. Shechem was a natural administrative center for the northern highland; Penuel provided a Transjordanian foothold. The building activity is the narrative marker of a king establishing his territorial claim (cf. Solomon’s building program, ch5-9).
26: wayyōʾmer yārāḇʿām bĕlibbô — Jeroboam said in his heart. The internal deliberation — unspoken, private — is reported by the omniscient narrator as the source of the apostasy. The reasoning is politically rational: the temple cult in Jerusalem would draw pilgrims north-to-south, and repeated contact between the northern people and the Jerusalem-Judah establishment would erode Jeroboam’s political hold. His fear that “the kingdom will revert to the house of David” is the fear of covenant re-centering around the Davidic sanctuary, which from a purely political perspective was well-founded.
27: If the people go up to offer sacrifices in the house of YHWH in Jerusalem, their hearts will turn back to their lord (ʾădōnêhem) Rehoboam, king of Judah, and they will kill me and return to Rehoboam. The political logic is lucid: cultic loyalty produces political loyalty. Jeroboam correctly perceived that the Jerusalem temple was not merely a religious institution but the covenant center that embodied Davidic legitimacy. His alternative cult will be designed to prevent this gravitational pull. The tragedy is that his solution — alternative worship sites — will be the formal breach of covenant that triggers the judgment announced in ch14.
28: wayyiqqaḥ hamelek ʿēṣāh wayyaʿaś šĕnê ʿĕglê zāhāḇ — the king took counsel and made two golden calves. The golden calves (ʿêglê zāhāḇ) echo Aaron’s golden calf at Sinai (Exod 32:4) in the precise words: “These are your gods, O Israel, who brought you up from the land of Egypt.” The DtrH’s use of the Sinai apostasy formula here is deliberate: Jeroboam’s cult innovation is the Sinai apostasy replicated at the national level. The worship formula redefines the covenant — it addresses the same God (YHWH who brought Israel from Egypt) while providing an illegitimate image. The calves likely functioned as thrones or pedestals for the invisible YHWH (as in the Ark theology) rather than as idols per se, but the DtrH treats them as equivalent to pagan idolatry.
29: He set up one in Bethel and the other in Dan. The two sites are geographically strategic: Bethel in the south (near the Judahite border, on the main north-south route) and Dan in the far north (at the foot of Mount Hermon). Both were ancient sanctuaries — Bethel had patriarchal associations (Gen 28:10-22; 35:1-15) and Dan was an established northern cult center (Judg 18). The choice of these two sites rather than a new one is Jeroboam’s appropriation of existing sacred geography to give his cult the aura of ancient legitimacy. The DtrH’s judgment is that this legitimacy is fraudulent.
30: wayhî haddāḇār hazzeh lĕḥaṭṭāʾṯ — this became a sin (ḥēṭ/transgression, from ḥāṭāʾ, H2399). The narrator’s formula “this became a sin” is repeated throughout Kings in reference to Jeroboam’s golden calves (cf. 1 Kgs 14:16; 15:26,30; 2 Kgs 10:29) — it becomes the technical phrase for the founding apostasy of the northern kingdom. The people went as far as Dan — the far northern sanctuary — showing that the cult took hold broadly across the territory. The ḥāṭāʾt is both the transgression and its consequences — a semantic range that the MKT “sin” captures more fully than “mistake” or “error”.
31: Jeroboam made shrines (bêṯ bāmôṯ, high-place temples) and appointed priests from all sorts of people who were not Levites (miqṣôṯ hāʿām ʾăšer lōʾ-hāyû mibĕnê lēwî). The appointment of non-Levitical priests is a second covenant violation alongside the golden calves — it directly contravenes the Mosaic prescription that the Levitical tribe alone serve as priests (Num 3:5-10). bêṯ bāmôṯ (plural, high-place temples) suggests a network of shrines, not merely the two calf-sites. Jeroboam is constructing a comprehensive alternative cultic infrastructure: new sites, new symbols, new clergy.
32: Jeroboam instituted a festival on the fifteenth day of the eighth month, similar to the festival in Judah (kaḥag ʾăšer bîhûḏāh). The Feast of Tabernacles (sukkôṯ) was celebrated on the fifteenth of the seventh month (Lev 23:34); Jeroboam moved it to the eighth month. The narrator’s comparison to the Judahite festival shows the deliberate parallelism — Jeroboam is creating an alternative liturgical calendar that mirrors Jerusalem’s while being permanently offset from it. The calendar manipulation is ideologically potent: whoever controls the sacred calendar controls the community’s rhythms of covenant renewal.
33: baḥōḏeš ʾăšer-bādāʾ millibô — in the month he had devised from his own heart. The phrase bādāʾ millibô (invented/devised from his own heart) is the DtrH’s theological verdict: Jeroboam’s calendar change was human innovation without divine authorization. The word bādāʾ (H908) — to devise, invent, fabricate — is the term for unauthorized religious innovation, worship not commanded by YHWH. The contrast with the prescribed calendar of Leviticus is stark: YHWH commanded specific festivals on specific days; Jeroboam created his own. The narrator’s repeated emphasis on this — Jeroboam himself went up to the altar at Bethel, burned incense, and officiated — shows the king assuming priestly functions that also violate the Mosaic separation of royal and priestly roles.
